docs.title: ShopWIKI — CNC Manufacturing Knowledge Base
docs.text: Welcome to the internal knowledge base for our aerospace CNC operations. This wiki contains setup instructions, Git workflows, system guides, and job-specific work instructions — all version-controlled and regularly updated.

docs.title: 🧪 Notes

• This is a test wiki site used for demonstration and internal development purposes only.

  • It contains no ITAR-regulated, proprietary, or intellectual property data.

  • Updates should be committed via Git following our internal revision control policy.

docs.title: How to Set Up a Self-Hosted MkDocs Wiki with Git Integration
docs.text: Author: Brian F. Date: 2025-05-30 Purpose: Provide a repeatable guide to deploy and manage a self-hosted MkDocs-based wiki site using Git for version control.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
This guide describes how to:

  • Host an internal documentation wiki using MkDocs
  • Version-control all content using Git
  • Serve the static site over your internal network
  • Automatically rebuild and deploy the site after Git pushes

This setup is intended for internal-only use and contains no ITAR or proprietary data.

docs.title: Server
docs.text:
• Linux server (e.g., Ubuntu or Rocky Linux)
  • SSH access
  • Python 3.8+
  • nginx or Apache for static file hosting

docs.title: Software
docs.text:
• MkDocs: static site generator
  • Git: version control

docs.title: 1. Create a Git Repository (Bare)
docs.text:
On the server:

mkdir -p /srv/git/wiki.git
cd /srv/git/wiki.git
git init --bare

docs.title: 2. Create a Working Directory
docs.text:
This is where the MkDocs build will happen:

mkdir -p /srv/wiki-working
cd /srv/wiki-working
git clone /srv/git/wiki.git .

docs.title: 3. Set Up MkDocs
docs.text:
Still inside /srv/wiki-working:

pip install mkdocs mkdocs-material
mkdocs new .

Customize your mkdocs.yml and add documentation to the docs/ folder.

docs.title: 4. Create a Git Hook to Auto-Build
docs.text:
In /srv/git/wiki.git/hooks/post-receive:

#!/bin/bash
GIT_WORK_TREE=/srv/wiki-working GIT_DIR=/srv/git/wiki.git git checkout -f
cd /srv/wiki-working
mkdocs build --clean
cp -r site/* /var/www/html/wiki

Make it executable:

chmod +x /srv/git/wiki.git/hooks/post-receive

docs.title: 5. Configure Web Server (e.g., nginx)
docs.text:
Example nginx block:

server {
    listen 80;
    server_name wiki.mycompany.local;
    root /var/www/html/wiki;
    index index.html;
    location / {
        try_files $uri $uri/ =404;
    }
}

Reload nginx:

sudo systemctl reload nginx

docs.title: 6. Push Changes from Your Workstation
docs.text:
From your local copy:

git remote add origin ssh://wiki-server:/srv/git/wiki.git
git push -u origin main

This triggers the post-receive hook and updates the site.

docs.title: Usage Notes
docs.text:
• Edit .md files locally using Zettlr or VS Code
  • Use mkdocs serve for live preview
  • Commit and push changes to deploy

docs.title: Troubleshooting
docs.text:
• Verify permissions on /srv/wiki-working and /var/www/html/wiki
  • Make sure post-receive is executable
  • Check logs in /var/log/nginx/ if the site won't load

docs.title: Optional Enhancements
docs.text:
• HTTPS via Let's Encrypt (internal cert authority)
  • Password-protected access via nginx or SSO
  • Git web interface (e.g., Gitea or GitWeb)

docs.title: Summary
docs.text: This guide sets up a version-controlled, internal documentation wiki suitable for CNC shops, manufacturing processes, or engineering references. It's fast, secure, and requires no external cloud services.

docs.title: 1. Repo / Directory Layout
docs.text:
Original 

ShopWIKI/
├── docs/
│   ├── index.md
│   └── ipad-restore.md
├── mkdocs.yml
└── .github/
    └── workflows/
        └── deploy.yml

Issue GitHub Pages 404’d and new pages (like our iPad guide) never appeared.

Final Either rename docs/ to the repo root or tell MkDocs where your docs live:

# mkdocs.yml
site_name: ShopWIKI
docs_dir: '.'        # if you want to store .md files at the repo root
theme:
  name: material
plugins:
  - search
  - awesome-pages

We kept our files in /docs, so we left the default and did not set docs_dir:.

docs.title: 2. mkdocs.yml Configuration
docs.text:
site_name: ShopWIKI

theme:
  name: material

plugins:
  - search
  - awesome-pages   # auto-builds the sidebar from your folder structure
  • site_name: shown in the navbar
  • plugins: include awesome-pages so new files drop into the nav automatically

docs.title: 3. Install Dependencies
docs.text:
pip install mkdocs-material mkdocs-awesome-pages-plugin
  • mkdocs-material: the Material theme
  • mkdocs-awesome-pages-plugin: auto-generates your sidebar

docs.title: 4. Local Preview
docs.text:
mkdocs serve
  • Open http://127.0.0.1:8000
  • Live-reload as you edit
  • Sidebar updates with every .md in docs/

docs.title: 5. GitHub Actions Workflow
docs.text:
Create or update .github/workflows/deploy.yml:

name: Deploy MkDocs
on:
  push:
    branches: [ main ]

jobs:
  build:
    permissions:
      contents: write       # allow pushing to gh-pages
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - uses: actions/setup-python@v5
        with:
          python-version: '3.x'

      - run: pip install mkdocs-material mkdocs-awesome-pages-plugin

      - run: mkdocs gh-deploy --force --verbose
  1. Checkout code
  2. Setup Python
  3. Install MkDocs + plugins
  4. Deploy to the gh-pages branch

docs.title: 6. Troubleshooting & Lessons
docs.text:
1. 404 on Pages

  2. Ensure GitHub Pages is set to serve from the gh-pages branch.

  3. “awesome-pages plugin not installed”

  4. Add mkdocs-awesome-pages-plugin to your pip install step.

  5. Local vs GitHub mismatch

  6. Commit & push every .md you add.

  7. Confirm docs_dir matches where your files live.

  8. Site Title wrong

  9. Update site_name in mkdocs.yml.

  10. Adding new pages

  11. With awesome-pages, just drop a .md into docs/.

  12. Or manually define in mkdocs.yml under nav: if you need custom order:

    yaml nav: - Home: index.md - iPad Restore: ipad-restore.md - MkDocs Guide: mkdocs-setup-guide.md

docs.title: 7. Next Steps
docs.text:
• Add front-matter (e.g. title:) to customize page titles.
  • Explore other plugins: redirects, include-markdown, etc.
  • When you’re ready, compare with GitBook or other hosted wikis—but this GitHub+MkDocs setup is rock-solid for now.

docs.title: Viewing Git Repositories Locally Without GitHub
docs.text: Author: Brian F. Date: 2025-05-30 Purpose: Provide easy, internal-only options for browsing Git repositories with or without a web interface.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
In a secure or air-gapped CNC environment, it's important to have simple, reliable ways to view Git commit history, diffs, and file changes without relying on GitHub or a full Git server like GitLab.

This guide explains several local tools for viewing Git history and comparing file changes. These methods work entirely offline and are suitable for internal documentation, CNC program versioning, and compliance tracking.

docs.title: Description
docs.text: Starts a temporary local website to browse your Git repo with a GUI, using the git instaweb command.

docs.title: Setup
docs.text:
Install Ruby (required by WEBrick):

sudo apt install ruby

Launch the viewer from any Git repo:

git instaweb --httpd=webrick

Then visit:

http://localhost:1234

To shut it down:

pkill -f instaweb

docs.title: Pros
docs.text:
• Built into Git
  • Lightweight
  • No server config needed

docs.title: Cons
docs.text:
• Basic UI
  • Requires Ruby

docs.title: Description
docs.text: tig is a full-screen terminal interface to Git. Great for CLI users.

docs.title: Installation
docs.text: sudo apt install tig

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: tig

docs.title: Features
docs.text:
• Browse commits, diffs, and file changes
  • Easily view logs and blame
  • Fully keyboard-driven

docs.title: Pros
docs.text:
• Fast, no dependencies
  • Great for quick log reviews

docs.title: Cons
docs.text: • Terminal only, no web UI

docs.title: 🌐 Option 3: Gitea (Optional Web Interface for Teams)
Description
docs.text: Gitea is a lightweight, self-hosted Git service with a GitHub-style web UI.

docs.title: Highlights
docs.text:
• Host Git repositories internally
  • Web-based editing, diffs, commit history
  • Great for multi-user teams

docs.title: Installation (optional, not required for local-only usage)
docs.text:
# Example Docker-based install
docker run -d --name=gitea -p 3000:3000 -v /srv/gitea:/data gitea/gitea:latest

docs.title: Pros
docs.text:
• Fully featured web UI
  • Good for future scaling

docs.title: Cons
docs.text: • Requires setup and server to run continuously

docs.title: ✅ Summary Table
docs.text: Tool Interface Setup Pros Use Case Git InstaWeb Web Low One-command viewer Browsing commits quickly tig Terminal Low Fast, rich terminal UI Engineers and techs at the bench Gitea Web High Full GitHub-style platform Teams, CI/CD, remote editing

docs.title: Recommendation
docs.text:
Start with git instaweb or tig for simplicity. If the team needs a full web interface later, consider deploying Gitea internally.

All tools listed here are fully offline and compliant with internal use in a secure manufacturing environment.

docs.title: Why Git is a Strategic Fit for Aerospace CNC Shops
docs.text: Author: Brian F. Company: [U.S. Aerospace Company] Date: 2025-05-30

docs.title: Introduction
docs.text: Git, originally designed for software development, is an ideal solution for managing change in complex, high-accountability environments. In aerospace CNC manufacturing, where precision, traceability, and compliance are non-negotiable, Git delivers powerful advantages over traditional file and folder management.

docs.title: Key Advantages of Using Git in an Aerospace CNC Shop
1. Full Revision History with Traceability
docs.text:
• Every change to a CNC program, setup sheet, or tooling list is logged.
  • Who made the change, when, and why — all tracked automatically.
  • Supports internal audits and external certification processes (AS9100, NADCAP).

docs.title: 2. No More Tribal Knowledge or File Chaos
docs.text:
• Eliminates shared drives with folders like FINAL_FINAL_2 or RevB_NEWNEW.
  • Git provides a clean commit history instead of overwriting or duplicating files.

docs.title: 3. Built-In Comparison Tools
docs.text:
• Instantly compare changes to G-code, Markdown setup sheets, and part documentation.
  • Example: git diff shows exactly what changed between RevA and RevB.

docs.title: 4. Rollbacks and Recovery
docs.text:
• Revert to a known-good version in seconds.
  • If a program crashes a machine, it’s easy to recover and analyze.

docs.title: 5. Supports Concurrent Development
docs.text:
• Engineers can safely work on new part revisions or improvements without impacting production.
  • Branching models allow development in parallel while keeping main stable.

docs.title: 6. Compatible with Secure On-Prem Hosting
docs.text:
• Git can run entirely offline or on a secure internal server.
  • Ideal for ITAR, DFARS, and CUI environments.

docs.title: 7. Audit-Friendly and Compliant
docs.text:
• Git logs are immutable.
  • Facilitates compliance with document control standards and version tracking requirements.

docs.title: 8. Open Source and Cost-Effective
docs.text:
• No licensing required.
  • Easy to integrate into existing systems and scale over time.

docs.title: Recommended Usage in the Shop
docs.text:
• Version control setup sheets, tooling sheets, and CNC code.
  • Use Markdown for human-readable setup documentation.
  • Host Git repositories internally (e.g., GitLab CE, bare SSH server).
  • Automate PDF exports or read-only dashboards for shop floor access.

docs.title: Conclusion
docs.text: Git brings engineering-grade control to the heart of manufacturing operations. By adopting Git for document and program versioning, aerospace CNC shops gain not only clarity and control but also compliance and agility. It's a low-cost, high-impact upgrade to any modern shop's digital infrastructure.

docs.title: Work Instruction System: Tool Sheets and CNC Programs with Git Version Control
docs.text: Project Title: Git-Based Tool Sheet and CNC Program Integration Author: Brian F. Company: [U.S. Aerospace Company] Date: 2025-05-30 Compliance: ITAR / DFARS / NIST 800-171 (CUI-Restricted Data)

docs.title: Objective
docs.text: To establish a compliant, version-controlled system that maintains accurate setup documentation, CNC programs, and tooling data through Git. This approach enhances traceability, reduces errors, and supports audit-readiness.

docs.title: Compliance Requirements
docs.text:
• Prohibit use of public Git repositories (e.g., GitHub.com)

  • Host Git repositories internally (e.g., GitLab, SSH-accessible server)

  • Ensure data remains within U.S. jurisdiction with access restrictions

  • Require commit history traceable to user and timestamp

docs.title: Tools Used
docs.text: Tool Purpose Git Version control for all files Zettlr Markdown editor for setup sheets Internal Git Server Secure on-prem Git access Markdown Portable and human-readable format CNC Machines Mazak (EIA format programs)

docs.title: Recommended Repository Structure
docs.text:
/tool-data/
├── 12345-Widget/
│   ├── RevA/
│   │   ├── setup-sheet.md
│   │   ├── op10_mainprogram.eia
│   │   ├── tool-list.csv
│   │   └── fixture-photo.jpg
│   ├── RevB/
│   │   ├── setup-sheet.md
│   │   ├── op10_mainprogram.eia
│   │   └── tool-list.csv
│   └── README.md

  • Each revision folder contains a complete set of related files

  • Operators may view or print the latest commit data

  • Engineers can inspect differences using git diff or revert when needed

docs.title: Checking the Latest Revision
docs.text:
1. Navigate to the appropriate part folder (e.g., tool-data/12345-Widget)

  2. Use git log or git status to verify you are working on the latest revision folder (e.g., RevB/)

  3. Confirm with the lead engineer if any uncertainty exists

docs.title: Editing the Setup Sheet
docs.text:
1. Open setup-sheet.md using Zettlr

  2. Make the required changes (e.g., clamp offset update, tool usage notes)

  3. Save the file

docs.title: Editing the CNC Program
docs.text:
1. Open the part file in Esprit CAM software

  2. Make applicable edits to the machining operations or post-processing parameters

  3. Post-process the program and export the updated .eia file

  4. Save the updated file into the appropriate revision folder

docs.title: Committing Changes to Git
docs.text:
cd tool-data/12345-Widget

git add RevB/
git commit -m "RevB: Updated clamp location and tool #12 feedrate"
git push origin main

docs.title: Common Git Commands and Examples
docs.text: These commands support traceability and review of documentation and code changes:

docs.title: View commit history
docs.text:
git log --oneline --graph

Example Output:

* a8cd9f2 RevB: Updated clamp location and tool #12 feedrate
* 4fa23d1 RevA: Initial release of part 12345-Widget

docs.title: View file-level edit history
docs.text:
git log -- setup-sheet.md

Example Output:

commit a8cd9f2a1b24c0fa5e9f4d1a28c7f92b3b7d2923
Author: Brian F. <brian@example.com>
Date:   Mon May 27 14:21:36 2025 -0500

    RevB: Updated clamp location and tool #12 feedrate

docs.title: Compare two revisions
docs.text:
git diff RevA RevB

Example Output:

--- a/RevA/setup-sheet.md
+++ b/RevB/setup-sheet.md
@@ -12,7 +12,7 @@
- Clamp offset set to 1.00 in from edge
+ Clamp offset updated to 1.25 in from edge per operator feedback

docs.title: View line-by-line author info
docs.text:
git blame setup-sheet.md

Example Output:

a8cd9f2 (Brian F. 2025-05-27 14:21:36 -0500) Clamp offset updated to 1.25 in from edge per operator feedback
4fa23d1 (Brian F. 2025-05-25 09:12:02 -0500) Tool #12 RPM set to 3200

docs.title: Check current branch and file status
docs.text:
git status

Example Output:

On branch main
Your branch is up to date with 'origin/main'.

Changes to be committed:
  (use "git restore --staged <file>..." to unstage)
        modified:   RevB/setup-sheet.md

docs.title: Benefits
docs.text:
• Unified source of truth for each setup revision

  • Full visibility and traceability of all changes

  • Consistency across shifts and machines

  • Conforms to ITAR and NIST 800-171 standards

  • Ready for downstream automation, export, or dashboard integration

docs.title: iPad Restore on Linux — What Went Wrong and How We Fixed It
docs.text: Overview · Where We Went Wrong · DFU vs Recovery Mode · New Build Workflow · Key Takeaways · Resources

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: We wanted to wipe and reinstall iPadOS on an older iPad using only Linux. Sounds simple, but we ran into a string of very familiar pain-points: flaky cables, half-broken packages, USB-port weirdness, and confusion about DFU versus Recovery mode. This document captures those dead-ends and, more importantly, the repeatable fix using the latest open-source stack.

docs.title: Where We Went Wrong
docs.text:
Cable Chaos: Our first micro-USB → Lightning cord was a charge-only knock-off. It would power the iPad but never enumerate as a USB device, so lsusb stayed silent and all restore tools failed.

  • USB 3.0 Quirks: DFU negotiates only 480 Mb/s. Plugging into a blue USB 3.x port caused intermittent disconnects on our kernel.

docs.title: DFU vs Recovery Mode
docs.text: DFU (Device Firmware Update) is the only mode that lets you completely reflash the firmware, whereas Recovery Mode simply reinstalls the OS without touching the bootloader. If you’re seeing the little cable-to-computer icon on the iPad screen, that’s Recovery Mode—not DFU—so tools like libimobiledevice won’t work until you force it into DFU (hold Power+Home for exactly 10 s, then release Power only).

docs.title: Part Number: 1656952-1
docs.text: This is a conceptual instruction sheet for internal demonstration purposes.

docs.title: ✅ Overview
docs.text: This document outlines the step-by-step instructions for processing the part number 1656952-1. All processes must follow standard operating procedures (SOPs), and any deviation must be approved by the Manufacturing Engineer.

docs.title: 🛠 Required Tools & Materials
docs.text:
Tool ID Description Group No. Notes T-101 1/2" Carbide End Mill 5 Use coated tool only T-202 3/8" Spot Drill 12 90° included angle T-303 Hydraulic Vise Fixture — Verify clamp force

🔧 Ensure all tools are in calibration. Use tool preset sheets from ToolCrib-Station 3.

docs.title: 📏 Setup Instructions
docs.text:
1. Load Part
  2. Use hydraulic fixture T-303

  3. Confirm datum A-B-C aligns per print

  4. Zero Machine

  5. Set Z0 to top of finished surface

  6. Use tool length offset per machine sheet

  7. Run Operation 10 (Rough Mill)

  8. Use T-101
  9. Feed: 18 IPM, Speed: 4200 RPM

  10. Coolant: ON (flood)

  11. Spot Drill Holes

  12. Use T-202
  13. Depth: 0.050"
  14. Ensure spot diameter ≥ 0.375"

docs.title: 🔍 Inspection Requirements
docs.text:
Feature Method Tolerance Gage Used Overall Height Caliper (digital) 2.000" ± .005" Mitutoyo 0–6" Hole Ø Pin Gage Ø0.375" ± .001" Class ZZ Edge Radius Visual + Template R0.125 ± .015 Radius Template R Set

🧪 Use first-piece inspection form QF-22 after initial part run.

docs.title: ⚠ Safety & Notes
docs.text:
• Always wear ANSI Z87.1 approved safety glasses.
  • Remove chips between steps.
  • Do not leave the machine unattended during the first article run.
  • Report any discrepancies or tool wear to the Shift Lead.

docs.title: ✅ Final Sign-Off
docs.text:
Inspector Date Notes _______ ____ _________

Document prepared by Brian Fosbinder – May 2025
